original_abstract: This article explores the missional potential of the biblical books of Ezra and Nehemiah, viewed as a single, two-volume literary unit. We argue that the written Word of God acts as a transformative force within society, primarily through the mediation and witness of the church. This dynamic suggests that spiritual renewal is a necessary prerequisite for lasting social reconstruction. To build a robust foundation for this missiological reflection, we examine the text through historical, canonical, and exegetical lenses, specifically considering the role of sacred scripture in the transition from an oral to a literate culture. We conclude by offering missional guidelines intended to support the development of a contemporary and socially relevant theology of mission.
humanized_abstract: The missional potential in Ezra-Nehemiah This article focuses on the missional potential in the biblical books of Ezra and Nehemiah seen as a single, two-volume literary work. As we have seen, the written Word of God can impact society in transformation from a missionary perspective only through the mediation and witness of the church, an awareness that points to the spiritual renewal needed for any lasting social rebuilding. The study will focus on a hermeneutical level on a historical, canonical and exegetical analysis of the scriptural function behind the transition from oral to literate culture and with this basis draw up some missional conclusions to further develop a contemporary, relevant theology of mission.
